ground_truth.title: Inside the feature store powering real-time AI in Dropbox Dash
ground_truth.author: Jason Shang
ground_truth.main_content:
Dropbox Dash uses AI to understand questions about your files, work chats, and company content, bringing everything together in one place for deeper, more focused work. With tens of thousands of potential work documents to consider, both search and agents rely on a ranking system powered by real-time machine learning to find the right files fast. At the core of that ranking in Dash is our feature store, a system that manages and delivers the data signals features our models use to predict relevance.

Our goals and requirements included bridging our hybrid infrastructure split between on-premises and Spark-native cloud environments, handling massive parallel reads while meeting strict sub-100ms latency budgets, capturing user intent in real-time within seconds, and supporting both streaming and batch computation patterns.

After surveying the feature store landscape, Feast stood out for its clear separation between feature definitions and infrastructure concerns and its modular architecture. Our Feast-based architecture combines three key components: Feast for orchestration and serving APIs (rewritten in Go for concurrency and latency), Spark for large-scale computation and feature ingestion, and Dynovault for low-latency online serving with ~20ms client-side latency.

To meet Dash's sub-100ms latency requirements, we rewrote the feature serving layer in Go. Using lightweight goroutines, shared memory, and faster JSON parsing, the Go service handles thousands of requests per second while adding only ~5-10ms of processing overhead, consistently achieving p95 latencies in the ~25-35ms range.

For keeping features fresh, we built a three-part ingestion system: batch ingestion with intelligent change detection reduced write volumes from hundreds of millions to under one million records per run and cut update times from over an hour to under five minutes; streaming ingestion captures fast-moving signals in near-real time; and direct writes handle lightweight or precomputed features by bypassing batch pipelines entirely.

Key lessons learned: Python's concurrency model became a limiting factor for high-throughput workloads, and understanding access patterns mattered as much as infrastructure changes. By recognizing that only 1-5% of feature values change in a typical 15-minute window, we dramatically reduced write volumes and ingestion time.
with: Dropbox Dash uses AI to understand questions about your files, work chats, and company content, bringing everything together in one place for deeper, more focused work.
After surveying the feature store landscape—Feast, Hopsworks, Featureform, Feathr, Databricks, and Tecton—Feast stood out for two reasons.

To remove these constraints, we rewrote the feature serving layer in Go. Using lightweight goroutines, shared memory, and faster JSON parsing, the Go service delivers true concurrency without the coordination costs we hit in Python.
By recognizing that only 1–5% of feature values change in a typical 15-minute window, we were able to dramatically reduce write volumes and ingestion time.

These optimizations came together in a hybrid architecture that balances flexibility and performance: Feast for orchestration and consistency, Spark for large-scale computation, and Dynovault for low-latency online serving.
